hook: Over a thousand rusting locomotives and carriages rot on dead sidings at the city's western edge.
facts: An open-air dump of decommissioned Hellenic Railways (OSE) rolling stock on the western edge of Thessaloniki, in the Nea Ionia / Diavata area, considered the largest train cemetery in Greece.
More than 1,000 disused wagons and diesel locomotives sit immobile across roughly ten inactive railway tracks, spread along the line between the refineries and the Gallikos river bridge.
Rolling stock began accumulating from the 1960s-70s and large-scale abandonment dates from the 1980s; many units have stood for over 30 years.
Reported among the stock are antique carriages dating to 1895 that once ran the Thessaloniki-Istanbul (Salonique-Constantinople) line, and vehicles seized by German forces in WWII.

A restored dining car attributed to the Orient Express was retrieved from the site; OSE has announced plans to clear the rolling stock by the end of 2027, with historic units sent to the planned Piraeus railway museum.
Reached by city buses (51, 54, 54A) from Thessaloniki station, getting off after the E90 highway.
w: Out on the western edge, where the rails run dead by the refineries, more than a thousand retired locomotives and carriages rust in long silent rows. The railways simply parked their old stock here and walked away; weeds climb the wheels, graffiti coats the flanks. No fence, no signs. A train graveyard you wander at your own risk.

hook: A forgotten Gaudi-like folly of half-ruined stone fountains and a tunnel to nowhere, hidden behind a hospital.
facts: A small park of whimsical, half-ruined stone structures east of Thessaloniki's eastern Byzantine walls, behind (north of) the Agios Dimitrios hospital on the slope of Kastron street climbing to Ano Poli.
Built around 1904, it is cited as the only integrated example of 'fantastic architecture' (architecture douce / eclecticism) in Thessaloniki, its forms compared to Gaudi's Park Guell in Barcelona.

Surviving elements include an ornate fountain ringed by a tunnel, a cistern for collecting rainwater, a low gate leading to an underground space, and a raised seating area, over roughly 1,000-2,000 square metres.
The architect is unknown and the origin of the name is uncertain; it is registered as a monument by the Greek Ministry of Culture.
It was part of the large garden grounds of the Agios Dimitrios hospital and lies near the church of the Apostle Paul.
legend: One tradition holds the gardens were the retreat of an Ottoman general, Seifoulah Pasha, giving them their name.
Local rumour casts the site as a meeting place or initiation temple for Ottoman freemasons or Sufi mystics.
Some say the gardens marked the end of Thessaloniki's catacombs, beyond the old city limits.
w: A scruffy, half-wild park clings to the Ano Poli slope, strewn with crumbling walls, blind tunnels and stairways that climb to nowhere. Nobody quite agrees what the Pasha's Gardens were — an Ottoman estate, a mystic's retreat, a Masonic folly — and the doubt only deepens the hush. Wander up for the ruins, the views and the riddle.

hook: Excavators at the ancient forum unearthed a combined bath, tavern and brothel betrayed by phallic vessels and a clay dildo.
facts: Excavations at the Ancient Agora / Roman Forum of Thessaloniki uncovered a complex of interconnected structures - a circular bathhouse, a tavern on a lower level, and a hall - identified as a combined bath-tavern-brothel.
The complex is dated to the 1st century BC (Hellenistic / late Republican period) and sits within the two-terraced forum that fronts the upper side of Aristotelous Square.
The circular bathhouse measured nearly 25 feet (about 7.5 m) across and held around 25 individual baths plus separate hot- and cold-water pools.
Finds in the hall - a red vessel with a phallic spout, a clay dildo, and an embossed glass vessel depicting Aphrodite - led excavators to interpret the space as a brothel.

Archaeologist Polyxeni Veleni of the Museum of Thessaloniki led the identification; it was reported as the first such combined establishment found in northern Greece (comparable complexes are known at Ephesos and Delos).
The forum itself was dug up accidentally in the 1960s, with systematic excavation through the 1970s-80s by Aristotle University teams; an underground museum now occupies part of the site.
w: Under the Roman forum, archaeologists turned up something earthier than temples: a first-century pleasure house wired into the public baths, with dining rooms above and a tavern alongside. The finds were frank — phallic lamps, a clay implement best left to the imagination — sealed when fire brought it down. Ancient Thessaloniki, off duty.

n: The Haunted House on Vasilissis Olgas
hook: A tower-shaped mansion at No. 263, left unfinished for a century, anchors Thessaloniki's best-known ghost legend.
facts: A deteriorated, tower-shaped neoclassical mansion at Vasilissis Olgas 263 (postcode 54655), in the Ntepo (Depo) district on the road from central Thessaloniki toward Kalamaria.
Sources date its construction to between 1918 and 1922 (with 1919 commonly cited); it has remained in an unfinished state for nearly a century.
It stands among the row of historic neoclassical mansions of the former 'Exoches' (countryside villas) quarter along Vasilissis Olgas avenue.
It is one of the most widely retold urban legends of Thessaloniki, repeatedly named the city's 'haunted house'.
legend: Folklore says the second floor collapsed twice during construction for unexplained reasons.
Stories tie the house to a wealthy Ottoman bey said to have strangled one of his wives there.

Other tales claim it was used by the Gestapo as an interrogation and torture site, with screams and footsteps reported from the basement at night, and that those who tried to live in or demolish it met mysterious ends.
w: A gaunt, tower-topped mansion on Vasilissis Olgas that has stood unfinished for a century, every attempt to complete it supposedly thwarted by collapses and screams in the night. Built around 1919, it gathered tales of a murdered bride, a torture cell, a restless bey. Nobody's tamed it. Thessaloniki's most stubborn ghost story, in brick.

hook: The seven-tower citadel crowning the upper town, a Byzantine-Ottoman fortress that served as a notorious prison until 1989.
facts: A Byzantine and Ottoman fortress on the north-eastern corner of the acropolis of Thessaloniki, in Ano Poli; its name means 'Fortress of Seven Towers' though it actually has ten towers, likely named after the Yedikule fortress in Constantinople.
Its northern towers are attributed to late antiquity (possibly under Emperor Theodosius I, r. 379-395, with an alternative 9th-century dating), and the southern towers and walls to the 12th century.
An inscription records rebuilding in 1431 by Cavus Bey after the Ottoman conquest, in the month of Ramadan of the year 834 (Hijri).
It was converted into a prison in the 1890s (the prison appears on an 1899 city map) and held political prisoners under the Metaxas regime, the Axis occupation, the Greek Civil War and the junta.
The prison operated until 1989, when it relocated outside the city; the site passed to the Ministry of Culture and the Byzantine archaeology service.
Systematic archaeological study and restoration began in 1990, with the first restoration phase completed in 1995.
w: The seven-towered citadel crowning the Upper Town began as the acropolis keep, was rebuilt by the Ottomans as Yedi Kule, and spent its last century as a grim prison whose name still chills older locals. Rebetiko songs were written about its cells. The gates stand open now. Climb up for the ramparts and the weight of it all.

hook: An 1810 Sephardic merchants' inn around an octagonal hall, the only surviving Ottoman han of its kind in the city, now a creative and events hub.
facts: Built from 1810 (per Ottoman archives) by the Sephardic Jewish merchant Samuel Bensousan, who ran it as a han (inn / small caravanserai) on Edessis Street in the upper Ladadika quarter.
Rooms for travelling traders were arranged around a central octagonal entrance hall; the cellar stored goods and the basement housed livestock.
It survived the Great Thessaloniki Fire of 1917 and continued as an inn until around 1930.
Through the 20th century it served successively as a spice/exotic-food store, fabric shop and coffee house, ending as a customs office at the end of the 1970s.
It is regarded as one of the last surviving Ottoman-era hans in Thessaloniki and today operates as a multidisciplinary cultural venue hosting art and events.
w: One of the last Ottoman-era hans in the city, this 1810 merchants' inn stored goods and animals below and lodged travellers above, raised by a Sephardic Jewish family in the commercial quarter. It came through the great fire of 1917 and decades of neglect, and now buzzes again as an arts space. Trade, ruin, revival, under one courtyard.

hook: A 1902 eclectic mosque built for the city's crypto-Jewish Donmeh community, later its first archaeological museum, now a municipal exhibition hall.
facts: Designed by Italian architect Vitaliano Poselli and completed in 1902 at 30 Archaiologikou Mouseiou Street, east of Vasilissis Olgas Avenue in the Exoches/Hamidiye district.
It was the last mosque built in Thessaloniki and the only one in the affluent villa quarter of Exoches, built as a place of worship for the Donmeh (Sabbatean crypto-Jewish converts to Islam).
Its two-storey eclectic facade blends neo-Moorish, neoclassical and Art Nouveau elements, with a marble sundial on the south side.
After the 1923-24 Greek-Turkish population exchange it briefly housed refugees, then served as Thessaloniki's Archaeological Museum from 1925 until 1963.
It is owned by the Municipality of Thessaloniki and used as an exhibition centre; in the 2020s (e.g. Eid 2024 and 2025) it has reopened occasionally for Muslim festivals.
w: Part mosque, part neoclassical villa, with a sundial and Stars of David worked into the stone — Yeni Cami went up in 1902 for the Donmeh, followers of a Jewish would-be messiah who had converted, outwardly, to Islam. It later served as the archaeological museum and now holds exhibitions. A building as layered as the city's faiths.

hook: The only surviving Byzantine bathhouse in Thessaloniki, a brick-vaulted hammam in continuous use for some seven centuries.
facts: Built in the late 12th or early 13th century, it stands at the corner of Theotokopoulou and Krispou streets in Ano Poli (Upper Town).
It is the only surviving Byzantine bath in Thessaloniki and the largest and most complete of the few Byzantine baths surviving in Greece.
Bathers passed in sequence through the rectangular frigidarium (cold dressing rooms), two vaulted tepidaria, and finally two caldaria (hot rooms).
In the Ottoman period it operated under the name Koule Hamam, and it remained in use as a public bath until 1940.
It is part of the Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1988, and is now open as a restored monument.
w: The only Byzantine bathhouse still standing in the eastern Mediterranean sits modestly in Ano Poli, its domes pierced with star-shaped vents for the steam-light. Built around 1300, it kept washing Thessalonians — Christian, then Muslim, then Christian again — right up to 1940. Step inside. Eight centuries of other people's baths.

hook: A protected pocket of 28 identical interwar single-storey houses with rose gardens and old tram-line traces, a 'time machine' wedged among apartment blocks.
facts: Built in 1927 by David Ouziel, a contractor and shareholder of the Thessaloniki tramway company, to designs by architect Jacques (Zak) Moshe.
It comprises 28 identical single-storey houses, arranged in groups of four along three sloping paved walkways off Georgiou Papandreou Avenue in the Depo area.
Intended to house tramway workers, the houses were ultimately never settled by them; the area is named 'Depo' after the former tram depot beside it.
The houses feature great ceiling height, panelled wooden doors, oblong windows and plaster ceiling rosettes, and many retain small private gardens.
The settlement has been a listed conservation area since 1984 and sits between Villa Allatini and Villa Morpurgo on the city's eastern villa belt.
w: A surviving pocket of low interwar cottages on a Depo avenue, the Ouziel quarter holds on as a fragment of the garden-suburb Thessaloniki that the apartment blocks otherwise swallowed. Built for refugees and named for a local family, its little houses keep rose-scented yards and a village hush. Walk the lane and the megacity falls away.

hook: A 1937 Art Deco tobacco warehouse, shuttered for decades, reborn as a boutique hotel that keeps its industrial bones.
facts: The building was erected in 1937 as the Gavriiloglou tobacco warehouse, one of the interwar tobacco-trade buildings of central Thessaloniki.
The tobacco warehouse closed in 1960 and the building stood disused for decades.
Its facade is a listed Work of Art protected by the Greek Ministry of Culture for its Art Deco and Expressionist character, with geometric patterns and strict symmetry.
It was converted into the 45-room Vanoro Hotel, which welcomed its first guests in 2021.
The conversion retains industrial detailing alongside an earthy, minimal interior, and includes the Anza Kitchen & Bar and a spa/wellness centre.
w: Thessaloniki grew rich on tobacco, and this hulking 1937 warehouse once stored the leaf by the ton before falling silent. In 2021 it reopened as a boutique hotel that kept its Art Deco bones — brick shell, iron, industrial scale — as decor. A monument to the trade that built the city, now with room service. Heritage you can sleep in.

hook: A 16th-century double Ottoman bathhouse, the 'Jewish bath', whose domes still anchor the flower-market square of Louloudadika.
facts: An Ottoman bathhouse built in the 16th century at the intersection of Komninon and Vasileos Irakleiou streets in the Louloudadika district.

It was originally called Pazar Hamami ('market bath') for its position in the heart of the bazaar; its later name Yahudi Hamam ('Jewish bath') comes from the largely Jewish population of the surrounding quarter.
It is a double bath, with separate men's and women's sections, each centred on a large domed hall surrounded by smaller domed chambers.
It was badly damaged in the Great Fire of 1917 and ceased operating as a bath in the early 20th century.

The surrounding square is known to locals as Louloudadika ('flower shops') after the florists that clustered around it; the monument's exterior is restored and floodlit while its interior is not regularly open to visitors.
w: A 16th-century Ottoman bathhouse anchors the Louloudadika, the old flower market that still perfumes the lanes around it. Built as a double bath for men and women, it was long called the Yahudi Hamam — the Jewish bath — for the Sephardic quarter it served. Domes, marble basins, ghosts of steam. Come for the bath, stay for the flowers.

hook: A hidden interwar refugee-built courtyard of antique shops that has become a beloved warren of ouzo tavernas and student bars.
facts: A small square of low buildings just north of Aristotelous and beside the Roman Forum, bounded by Olympou and Filippou streets and threaded by the diagonal Tositsa Street.
It was laid out in 1928 as a refugee settlement for Greeks displaced by the 1922 Asia Minor Catastrophe.
Its name (from the Turkish 'bit bazaar', flea market) recalls the second-hand goods, including lice-ridden used clothes, once sold there.
Ground-floor shops around the central courtyard still trade in antiques, old furniture and bric-a-brac.
Since the 2000s the square has filled with mezedopoleia (ouzo tavernas), cafes and alternative bars and become a nightlife hub popular with students.
w: A ramshackle square of antique shops and tavernas built by refugees in 1928, the Bit Bazaar trades junk and treasure by day and fills with rebetiko, ouzo and students by night. Once the city's flea market, it has slid into something between junk shop and open-air bar. Hunt for a typewriter, stay for the meze. Gloriously unpolished.

hook: A tiny Ano Poli chapel guarding a luminous late-antique apse mosaic of a youthful, beardless Christ in glory.
facts: Late 5th-century church that served as the katholikon of the Byzantine Latomos (Latomou) Monastery.
Its apse holds a vividly preserved early Byzantine mosaic, the 'Icon of Christ of Latomos', showing a beardless Christ enthroned in a mandorla flanked by the prophets Ezekiel and Habakkuk.
Mosaic and fresco decoration was renewed in the 12th-14th centuries; surviving wall paintings are of high quality.
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1988 as one of fifteen Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki (ref. 456).
It is one of the few Thessaloniki churches to preserve mosaic decoration from the early Byzantine period.
legend: Tradition holds the apse mosaic is an acheiropoieton, an image believed to have appeared miraculously rather than by human hand.
Legend says the mosaic was hidden behind plaster or leather for centuries and was rediscovered when it was revealed by a miracle.
w: A tiny stone chapel in the Ano Poli lanes guards a miracle in gold: a shimmering 6th-century mosaic of a young, beardless Christ enthroned on the rainbow. Legend says it lay hidden behind plaster for centuries, revealed by an earthquake. The hermit David supposedly lived nearby in an almond tree. Pure, luminous Byzantium.

n: Church of Saint Nicholas Orphanos (Agios Nikolaos Orfanos)
hook: A modest garden-hidden church near the eastern wall whose interior is sheathed in some of the finest Palaiologan frescoes anywhere.
facts: Early 14th-century church standing just inside the eastern Byzantine wall of the old city, between Irodotou and Apostolou Pavlou streets.
Its interior decoration dates the building to c. 1310-1320, at the height of the 'Palaiologan Renaissance' of the Thessalonican school.
The frescoes cover nearly all interior surfaces and rank among the most important painted ensembles of the early 14th century.
The wall paintings were uncovered during restoration works in 1957-1960.
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1988 as part of the Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki.
legend: Scholars have suggested a link to Serbian King Stephen Uros II Milutin and to the painter of the Hilandar Monastery on Mount Athos, but this patronage remains a hypothesis rather than documented fact.
w: Hidden behind a leafy garden wall in the Upper Town, this small 14th-century church wraps its walls in some of the finest frescoes of the late Byzantine flowering — saints and scenes so vivid they almost narrate themselves. It slipped through the Ottoman centuries as a quiet parish. Push the gate and step into a painted world.

hook: A graceful late-Byzantine brick church in the western Upper Town, its outer walls patterned in decorative brickwork.
facts: Late Byzantine (Palaiologan) church in the northwestern corner of the Ano Poli; its exact date and original dedication are unknown.
Surviving fragments of interior fresco decoration are dated to c. 1315.
Scholars have suggested it may have been the katholikon of the Monastery of Christ the Almighty (Pantodynamos).
Converted into a mosque by Yakup Pasha under Ottoman sultan Bayezid II (r. 1481-1512) and known as the Yakup Pasha Mosque.
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1988 (ref. 456-011) as part of the Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki.
w: A jewel of late-Byzantine brickwork stands quietly in the western Upper Town, its outer walls patterned in herringbone and recessed arches like needlework in stone. Raised around 1300 and made a mosque under the Ottomans, Saint Catherine's kept its grace through it all. UNESCO-listed, and almost always blissfully empty.

hook: An atmospheric underground vault beneath the basilica of the city's patron saint, traditionally the site of his imprisonment and martyrdom.
facts: Lies beneath the sanctuary and transept of the Basilica of Hagios Demetrios, the church of Thessaloniki's patron saint.
Archaeology and tradition identify the space as a Roman bathhouse where Saint Demetrios was imprisoned and martyred in AD 303; rising ground levels over the centuries turned it into a crypt.
It filled with earth under Ottoman rule and was rediscovered only after the great fire of 1917, with excavations in the 1930s-1940s revealing Roman baths and a well.
Now an exhibition space across several rooms displaying sculptures, capitals, closure slabs, inscriptions and the remaining hexagonal ciborium of the church.
Distinct from the basilica above: the basilica is the upper Paleochristian church (UNESCO-listed), while the crypt is a separate underground martyrium-museum with its own entrance and exhibits.
legend: The crypt is venerated as the exact spot of Saint Demetrios's martyrdom; the precise location of his imprisonment rests on tradition as much as archaeology.
w: Beneath the great basilica of the city's patron lies the crypt where, by tradition, the soldier-saint Demetrios was held and martyred in Roman baths. Rediscovered after the fire of 1917, the underground halls now show columns, fountains and fragments of sixteen centuries. Cool, dim, half-forgotten. Descend to where the story began.

hook: The only surviving Byzantine monastery in Thessaloniki, a working community high in the Upper Town with sweeping views over the city and gulf.
facts: Founded in the mid-14th century (c. 1351), traditionally associated with the empress Anna Palaiologina and named after the monks Dorotheos and Markos Vlattis, friends of Saint Gregory Palamas.
Originally dedicated to Christ Pantokrator and known as the Monastery of the Pantokrator of the Vlatadon.
Its main church was converted into a mosque under Ottoman rule and its interior frescoes plastered over; the monastery nonetheless survived continuously.
It is the only Byzantine-era monastery in Thessaloniki to survive to the present day.
Today it also houses the Patriarchal Institute for Patristic Studies and is inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List (1988) with the city's other Byzantine monuments.
legend: Local tradition associates a spot in the grounds with the Apostle Paul preaching to the Thessalonians, but this is devotional lore rather than documented history.
w: A working monastery high on the Upper Town slope, founded in the 14th century and still home to monks, peacocks and one of the best balconies over the city and gulf. Tradition holds the Apostle Paul preached on this very spot. Its little church keeps faded frescoes; its terrace keeps the view. Calm above the rooftops, and free to enter.

hook: A compact late-13th/early-14th-century Byzantine church near the Rotunda and Kamara, of the elegant cross-in-square type.
facts: Late 13th / early 14th-century Byzantine church in the eastern part of the old city, at the corner of Iasonidou and Arrianou streets near the Rotunda of Galerius.
Identified with the Byzantine Monastery of the Theotokos Peribleptos, also known as the Monastery of Kyr Isaak after its founder, Jacob, Metropolitan of Thessaloniki (1295-1315).
Of the tetrastyle cross-in-square architectural type, with a narthex and a now-destroyed ambulatory connecting to two surviving chapels.
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1988 as part of the Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki.
w: On a corner near the Arch of Galerius stands a graceful late-13th-century church, its brick domes and tiled roofs a textbook of the Palaiologan style. Once the heart of a larger monastery, Agios Panteleimon became a mosque, then a church again, losing and regaining its frescoes. Small, serene and easy to miss. Byzantine poise in stone.

n: Church of the Prophet Elijah (Profitis Ilias)
hook: A tall, finely domed late-14th-century church of the Athonite type, built over a palace ruined in the Zealot revolt.
facts: Late 14th-century Byzantine church in the Ano Poli, at the junction of Olympiados and Amphilochias streets.
Built on the ruins of a palace devastated by the Zealots during the uprising of 1342; its original dedication is unknown.
Displays the Athonite (triconch) architectural type with a fine dome and ceramic decoration that influenced later Serbian church building.
In Ottoman times it was the Sarayli (Palace/Court) Mosque; the modern dedication to the Prophet Elijah arose through a misreading of that name.
Inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1988 as part of the Paleochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessaloniki.
w: A commanding 14th-century church on the Upper Town's spine, built to the Athonite plan of the great Mount Athos monasteries, its high brick masses unusual here. Profitis Ilias served as a mosque under the Ottomans, who whitewashed its frescoes; patches survive. Climb to it for the architecture and the quiet square outside.

hook: Post-Byzantine church on Egnatia whose 1841 carved iconostasis is crowned by a Cross flanked by dragons alluding to the story of the Prophet Jonah.
facts: Dedicated to the Hypapante (Presentation of Christ in the Temple); the present building results from a radical renovation of 1841.
Stands on the corner of Agapinou and Egnatia streets in central Thessaloniki, near the Kamara (Arch of Galerius) and opposite Panagia Dexia.
Its carved wooden iconostasis dates to 1841 and is topped by a Cross with dragons beneath it that allude to the Jonah narrative.
Houses portable icons made in Thessaloniki and Mount Athos workshops, plus a 16th-century sanctuary door attributed to the Cretan School.
Interior icons span the 16th, 18th and 19th centuries.
w: A 19th-century church near Kamara worth entering for one thing: its towering wooden iconostasis, carved into a riot of vines, beasts and biblical scenes, with Jonah's whale coiling like a dragon beneath the Cross. The woodcarvers of the 1840s let rip. Easy to pass on busy Egnatia. Step in and look up at the carpentry.

hook: A 1727 post-Byzantine basilica raised on the ruins of a 12th-century Byzantine monastery, with a gilded Greek-Baroque iconostasis.
facts: Dedicated to the Dormition of the Mother of God; the current edifice was built in 1727.
It stands on the site of a 12th-century Byzantine monastery to the Virgin that burned down around 1690.
Located at the junction of Dimitriou Gounari and Mitropoleos streets in central Thessaloniki (OSM display address: Dimitriou Gounari).
It is a three-aisled basilica with a gynaikonitis (women's gallery) and a western colonnade.
Its carved wooden iconostasis was gold-plated in 1763 and ranks among the finest examples of Greek Baroque woodcarving in the city.
The ceiling murals date to the 19th century.
w: Raised in 1727 on the burnt bones of a 12th-century monastery, this church carries its layered history in its name — the 'New' Madonna. Inside hang dark old icons and an incense-soaked grandeur; outside, the city roars past. Destroyed, rebuilt and renamed, it embodies the Thessalonian habit of starting over. Stitched from its own ruins.

hook: The city's smallest Byzantine church, affectionately called 'Sotirakis' (little Sotiris); a tiny 14th-century domed chapel that was never converted to a mosque.
facts: A small mid-14th-century Byzantine church (dated to around 1350 from a coin found in its dome), one of the smallest in Thessaloniki.
Dedicated to the Transfiguration of the Saviour (Metamorfosis tou Sotiros), feast of 6 August; locals call it 'Sotirakis', little Sotiris.
Inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List as part of the Palaeochristian and Byzantine Monuments of Thessalonika (1988).
Stands on Egnatia near the Kamara, between the churches of Hypapante and Panagouda, at the junction with Paleon Patron Germanou.
Owing to its small size it was never converted into a mosque during Ottoman rule; its dome is unusually tall for the building.
Its interior preserves 14th-century wall-paintings dated to about 1350-1370.
w: Barely bigger than a chapel, this little domed church of around 1350 squats below street level on Egnatia, dwarfed by the blocks around it. Coins in its walls date it; locals fondly call it 'little Sotiris.' Its eight-sided dome and surviving frescoes pack the whole Byzantine vocabulary into a miniature. UNESCO-listed, and overlooked.

hook: The 1818 'Panagouda' church, a small-domed basilica with a Pantokrator fresco in its dome and a neoclassical bell tower.
facts: Officially the Church of Panagia Gorgoepikoos, known as Panagouda; built in 1818 to replace an earlier church that burned the year before.
Located at the junction of Egnatia with Paleon Patron Germanou street (OSM: Ieros Naos Panagoudas, Paleon Patron Germanou).
It has a small dome decorated with Christ Pantokrator and the Apostles and is fronted by a neoclassical bell tower.
It is a three-naved basilica with a zenana (women's gallery), an architectural type widespread in Macedonia under Ottoman rule in the 19th century.
It was the centre of a Christian neighbourhood during the Ottoman period and is dedicated to the Birth of the Virgin.
w: Rebuilt in 1818 after fire, this warm old parish church hides a glowing Pantokrator gazing down from its dome and a neoclassical bell tower outside. It long served as the seat of the city's bishops and still draws a devoted congregation. Gilded icons, worn flagstones, candle wax. A living church rather than a museum piece, on Egnatia.

hook: An early-19th-century post-Byzantine, three-aisled wooden-roofed basilica, one of the oldest surviving of its kind in the city.
facts: Built in 1818 to replace an older church destroyed by fire the previous year; one of the oldest surviving post-Byzantine churches in Thessaloniki.
A three-aisled wooden-roofed basilica with interior dimensions of about 18.55 by 15.35 metres, divided by two rows of six pillars.
The central aisle is dedicated to Agios Athanasios, the right aisle to Agios Eleftherios and the left aisle to the Three Hierarchs.
Located at the corner of Egnatia (no. 87) and Sokratous streets, less than 100 m from the church of Panagia Acheiropoietos.
The interior preserves a despotic throne, a pulpit and a wooden iconostasis with notable icons including an Annunciation of the Virgin.
w: An early-19th-century basilica wedged among the shops off Egnatia, three-aisled and timber-roofed in the modest post-Byzantine manner forced on Christians under Ottoman rule. No dome, no grand facade — just a low, calm interior of dark wood and old icons. The kind of working church locals slip into between errands. Easily missed.

hook: A purpose-built art museum on the edge of the university campus, with a panoramic view over the Thermaic Gulf to Mount Olympus.
facts: Art museum and gallery of the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, founded on the bequest of Nestor and Aliki Teloglou.
Located at Agiou Dimitriou 159A, 54636 Thessaloniki, on the outskirts of the university campus (within bbox lng 22.85-23.05, lat 40.58-40.7).
Occupies a modern purpose-built building set in a large green park, with a panoramic view over the Thermaic Gulf and Mount Olympus.
Holds and exhibits collections of fine art and hosts temporary exhibitions and educational programmes.
w: A serious art museum hiding by the university campus, the Teloglion grew from the bequest of a tobacco magnate and his wife, who gave their collection and fortune. Greek modern masters, Asian art and old engravings fill its calm galleries, with sharp temporary shows. Far from the tourist trail and better for it. Connoisseur territory.
